title: Why a city square makes people slow down
summary: Describe and explain why a particular public square or plaza changes how people move and behave, using present perfect and modals.
topic: The effect of a city square on people's behaviour
prompt: Write 3 paragraphs (400–500 words) describing a city square you know or can imagine, and explaining why it makes people move more slowly and stay longer than they planned. Use the present perfect to describe what the space has done to you or others, and at least one modal to talk about what such spaces could or should do for a city.
hintStarters: The first time I walked through this square, I had not expected to…
What strikes me most is the way the space has…
If more city planners understood this effect, they would…

hintVocab.meaningVi: nán lại, dừng chân lâu hơn
tập hợp một cách tự phát
làm dịu các góc cạnh của
cung cấp nơi trú ẩn, bóng mát
mời gọi mọi người ngồi xuống

hintVocab.phrase: to watch the world go by
hintVocab.meaningVi: ngồi ngắm cuộc sống qua lại

sampleText:
There is a square near the old university in Porto that I have walked through dozens of times, and it has never once let me pass quickly. I always arrive intending to cross it in thirty seconds, and I always leave ten or fifteen minutes later, without quite knowing how the time went. The square itself is not large. It is surrounded on three sides by low stone buildings with arched doorways, and in the centre there is a fountain that no longer works but still holds water from the last rain. A few metal benches face the fountain at angles that feel accidental but must have been carefully chosen. The whole space is perhaps fifty metres across. There is one tree, planted off-centre, which provides a small patch of shade in the afternoon and, for reasons I cannot explain, makes the whole space feel more balanced.

What the square has done, over years of small adjustments, is remove every reason to hurry. There are no roads running through it. The stone underfoot is uneven in a way that makes you look down and slow your pace automatically. The buildings at the edges block the wind and most of the traffic noise, so the first thing you notice when you enter is that the city has become quieter. Once the noise drops, you tend to notice the details — a tile missing from a wall, pigeons arguing over crumbs, an old man reading a folded newspaper on the nearest bench. These things were always there, but you could not see them while moving fast. The square does not ask anything of you except that you enter it honestly, on foot.

A square like this could teach city planners something that no building regulation quite captures. It is not the size of the space that matters, nor the quality of the materials. What makes people slow down is the combination of enclosure, a reason to look at something, and the physical sensation that rushing would be slightly wrong here — the way an uneven floor politely insists on your attention. If every new development in a city included one small square with these qualities, the overall mood of the streets nearby would change. Spaces like this have proved, in cities as different as Seville and Chiang Mai and Oaxaca, that people do not need to be told to slow down. They simply need a space that makes speed feel like the stranger option.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Present perfect for repeated past experience
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'I have walked through dozens of times' và 'it has never once let me pass quickly' dùng hiện tại hoàn thành để diễn đạt kinh nghiệm lặp đi lặp lại cho đến tận bây giờ — không phải một lần cụ thể trong quá khứ.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'I have walked through dozens of times' and 'it has never once let me pass quickly' use the present perfect to describe repeated experience up to the present, not a single specific past event.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Modal 'could' to draw a lesson
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'A square like this could teach city planners…' dùng 'could' để đề xuất một khả năng hoặc bài học tiềm ẩn — không chắc chắn như 'should', nhưng vẫn mang tính thuyết phục trong văn nghị luận.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'A square like this could teach city planners…' uses 'could' to suggest a potential lesson — less assertive than 'should' but still persuasive in argumentative writing.

sampleTranslationVi:
Có một quảng trường gần trường đại học cổ ở Porto mà tôi đã đi qua hàng chục lần, và nó chưa bao giờ để tôi đi qua nhanh được. Tôi luôn đến với ý định đi xuyên qua nó trong ba mươi giây, nhưng luôn rời đi mười hoặc mười lăm phút sau, mà không biết thời gian đã trôi qua như thế nào. Bản thân quảng trường không lớn. Nó được bao quanh ở ba mặt bởi những tòa nhà đá thấp với những cổng vòm, và ở giữa có một đài phun nước không còn hoạt động nữa nhưng vẫn giữ nước từ trận mưa cuối cùng. Vài chiếc ghế kim loại hướng về đài phun nước ở những góc độ trông như tình cờ nhưng chắc chắn đã được lựa chọn cẩn thận. Toàn bộ không gian rộng khoảng năm mươi mét. Có một cái cây, được trồng lệch tâm, cung cấp một mảng bóng mát nhỏ vào buổi chiều và, vì lý do tôi không thể giải thích, làm cho toàn bộ không gian cảm thấy cân bằng hơn.

Điều mà quảng trường đã làm, qua nhiều năm điều chỉnh nhỏ, là loại bỏ mọi lý do để vội vàng. Không có con đường nào chạy xuyên qua nó. Mặt đá bên dưới gồ ghề theo cách khiến bạn phải nhìn xuống và tự động giảm tốc độ bước chân. Những tòa nhà ở rìa chắn gió và hầu hết tiếng ồn giao thông, vì vậy điều đầu tiên bạn nhận ra khi bước vào là thành phố đã trở nên yên tĩnh hơn. Khi tiếng ồn giảm bớt, bạn có xu hướng chú ý đến các chi tiết — một viên gạch bị vỡ trên tường, những con bồ câu tranh nhau mẩu bánh vụn, một cụ ông đọc tờ báo gấp trên chiếc ghế gần nhất. Những thứ này vẫn luôn ở đó, nhưng bạn không thể nhìn thấy chúng khi đang di chuyển nhanh. Quảng trường không đòi hỏi gì ở bạn ngoại trừ việc bước vào một cách thành tâm, bằng đôi chân của mình.

Một quảng trường như thế này có thể dạy các nhà quy hoạch đô thị điều mà không một quy định xây dựng nào nắm bắt được hoàn toàn. Không phải kích thước của không gian quan trọng, cũng không phải chất lượng vật liệu. Điều khiến mọi người chậm lại là sự kết hợp của không gian bao bọc, một lý do để nhìn vào điều gì đó, và cảm giác vật lý rằng vội vàng ở đây sẽ có gì đó không ổn — cái cách mà nền nhà gồ ghề lịch sự đòi hỏi sự chú ý của bạn. Nếu mỗi khu phát triển mới trong thành phố có một quảng trường nhỏ với những phẩm chất này, bầu không khí chung của các con phố xung quanh sẽ thay đổi. Những không gian như thế này đã chứng minh, ở những thành phố khác nhau như Seville, Chiang Mai và Oaxaca, rằng mọi người không cần được bảo phải chậm lại. Họ chỉ cần một không gian khiến tốc độ trở thành lựa chọn kỳ lạ hơn.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses the present perfect correctly to describe a repeated experience?
options: I have walked through this square dozens of times.
I have walked through this square dozen of times.
I walked through this square dozens of times.
I have been walking through this square dozens of times ago.
mcQuestions.explanation: Present perfect with 'dozens of times' describes repeated experience up to now. 'Walked' (past simple) removes the connection to the present. 'Dozens' not 'dozen' is the correct form here. 'Ago' is incompatible with present perfect.
mcQuestions.hint: Look at the time expression and whether the experience extends to the present.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'no longer' to describe something that has stopped?
options: The fountain no longer works, but it still holds water.
The fountain no more works, but it still holds water.
The fountain not longer works, but it still holds water.
The fountain doesn't longer work, but it still holds water.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'No longer' is the standard phrase for something that was true before but is not now. 'No more' in this position is non-standard in formal writing. 'Not longer' and 'doesn't longer' are grammatically incorrect.
mcQuestions.hint: The fixed phrase for 'not anymore' placed before a verb is two words starting with 'no'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'tend to' correctly to describe a general habit?
options: When the noise drops, you tend to notice the details.
When the noise drops, you tend notice the details.
When the noise drops, you tend noticing the details.
When the noise drops, you are tend to notice the details.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Tend to' is followed by the bare infinitive: 'tend to + verb'. 'Tend' alone without 'to', or 'tend noticing', are ungrammatical. 'Are tend' is also wrong.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Tend to' must be followed by the base form of the verb.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'could' to suggest a potential lesson?
options: A square like this could teach city planners something important.
A square like this could to teach city planners something important.
A square like this could teaches city planners something important.
A square like this could taught city planners something important.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Could' (modal) is followed by the bare infinitive — never 'to + verb', never an inflected form like 'teaches' or 'taught'.
mcQuestions.hint: After a modal verb, use only the base form of the verb.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'surrounded by' correctly in a passive construction?
options: The square is surrounded by low stone buildings on three sides.
The square is surrounded with low stone buildings on three sides.
The square is surround by low stone buildings on three sides.
The square surrounds by low stone buildings on three sides.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Surrounded by' is the correct passive collocation — not 'surrounded with'. The past participle 'surrounded' is required, not the base 'surround'. The active voice 'surrounds by' is ungrammatical.
mcQuestions.hint: The fixed passive phrase uses 'by', and the participle form of the verb.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence best describes a physical effect on the body using the adverb 'automatically'?
options: The uneven stone makes you slow your pace automatically.
The uneven stone makes you to slow your pace automatically.
The uneven stone makes you slowing your pace automatically.
The uneven stone makes you slow your pace automatic.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Make + object + bare infinitive' — no 'to', no '-ing'. 'Automatic' is an adjective; 'automatically' (adverb) is correct here because it modifies the verb.
mcQuestions.hint: After 'make someone', use the bare infinitive; the word modifying the verb needs to be an adverb.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'neither … nor' correctly to reject two options?
options: Neither the size nor the quality of the materials determines the effect.
Neither the size or the quality of the materials determines the effect.
Neither the size nor the quality of the materials determine the effect.
Neither size nor the quality of the materials determines the effect.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Neither … nor' is the correct pair. 'Neither … or' is non-standard. When 'neither … nor' precedes two singular nouns, the verb is singular ('determines'). Both nouns need articles for this balanced construction.
mcQuestions.hint: The partner of 'neither' is 'nor', and the verb agrees with the nearest subject.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses the conditional 'if … would' to predict a result?
options: If every development included one small square, the mood of the streets would change.
If every development will include one small square, the mood of the streets would change.
If every development included one small square, the mood of the streets will change.
If every development included one small square, the mood of the streets would changed.
mcQuestions.explanation: Second conditional: 'If + past simple, … would + bare infinitive'. Mixing 'will' in either clause or using 'would changed' (past participle instead of base) are all errors.
mcQuestions.hint: Second conditional needs past simple in the 'if' clause and 'would + base verb' in the main clause.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'simply' correctly as an adverb of manner?
options: People simply need a space that makes speed feel strange.
People simply needs a space that makes speed feel strange.
People simply need a space that make speed feel strange.
People simply needed a space that makes speed feel strange.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'People' is plural so the verb is 'need' (not 'needs'). The relative clause's verb should agree with 'a space' (singular), so 'makes' is correct. Past tense 'needed' shifts meaning without reason.
mcQuestions.hint: Check subject-verb agreement for 'people' and then again for 'a space' in the relative clause.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which phrase most naturally describes overhearing the sounds of nature in a square?
options: pigeons arguing over crumbs
pigeons who are disputing about crumbs
pigeons that make argument for crumbs
arguing pigeons of crumbs
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Arguing over' is a natural participial phrase for describing competition. 'Disputing about' is correct grammar but sounds stiff for this concrete image. 'Make argument for' is non-native. The last option inverts the structure awkwardly.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the most natural-sounding participial phrase to describe competitive behaviour.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses the present perfect passive to describe what a space has done over time?
options: What the square has done, over years of small adjustments, is remove every reason to hurry.
What the square has did, over years of small adjustments, is remove every reason to hurry.
What the square have done, over years of small adjustments, is remove every reason to hurry.
What the square has done, over years of small adjustments, are remove every reason to hurry.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Has done' = present perfect with singular subject 'the square'. 'Has did' is wrong (past participle of 'do' is 'done'). 'Have done' needs a plural subject. The main verb after 'is' here is 'remove' (infinitive), not 'are remove'.
mcQuestions.hint: The subject is singular; present perfect needs 'has + past participle'.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Think of a place in your city or town where you often slow down or stop unexpectedly. What is it about the design of that place — rather than what is happening there — that causes you to linger?
